claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with the basics: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex, who is 15 years old. I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have a 15-year-old son named Alex. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And do you live with Alex, or does he live with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 15 years old and lives with you in the UK, you would normally qualify for Child Benefit.

Just a few more questions to make sure there are no circumstances that might affect eligibility:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations:**
- In hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks?
- In care (provided by the local council) for more than 8 weeks?
- Working 24 hours or more per week?
- Receiving benefits in his own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply. Alex isn't in hospital or care, he doesn't work that many hours, and he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. Though I should mention - I am fostering Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that you're fostering Alex. That's an important detail!

To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit while fostering Alex, I need to ask: **Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council for fostering him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Since you're fostering Alex and the local council is not paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance, you qualify for Child Benefit. Alex is 15 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and none of the disqualifying circumstances apply to him.

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Alex. If you haven't already done so, you can make a claim through the usual Child Benefit application process.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 15 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for him. According to the rules, you'll get Child Benefit if you foster a child, as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. Since the local council is not paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex. There are no other circumstances that would affect eligibility - Alex is not in hospital/care for extended periods, not working 24+ hours per week, and not receiving benefits in his own right.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering a 15-year-old child named Alex who lives with them in the UK. The local council is not paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. According to Child Benefit rules, foster carers qualify for Child Benefit as long as the local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met and no disqualifying circumstances apply.

According to the rules, you'll get Child Benefit if you foster a child, as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. Since the local council is not paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex. There are no other circumstances that would affect eligibility - Alex is not in hospital/care for extended periods, not working 24+ hours per week, and not receiving benefits in his own right.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering a 15-year-old child named Alex who lives with them in the UK. The local council is not paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. According to Child Benefit rules, foster carers qualify for Child Benefit as long as the local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met and no disqualifying circumstances apply.
